## [51:47 - 54:52]

agent who owns a truck or an FBI agent who loves his dog or an FBI agent who plays tennis on the weekends, you know, and if you learn how to sort of drip that information in, we're not going to tell someone who's barricaded themselves a five minute story. Right. But if we learn how to do that strategic release of information in a way that allows people to see us as human beings and not just the roles that we feel that can be really powerful. I'm teaching business people all the time that if you create or if you write a keynote that anyone can deliver, then no one should deliver it because it's going to be terrible. Every single time you are delivering a keynote or a strategy or an all hands, any kind of a business speaking opportunity, it should be tailored specifically to the person who is speaking so that they can become not a corporate figurehead or a spokesperson or a CEO, but a human being with human characteristics who also is presenting a product, an idea, an investment opportunity. So you have to be a person first, like a great Charlie Munger and Warren Buffett are great examples of this because they make sure we know who they are as human beings, as well as the greatest investors of all time. They're not just sort of innocuous people in the same way that if you think about your teachers of your life as when you were a student, I tend to think the best teachers we ever had were the ones who shared with us. I took four years of French, even though I didn't like French very much. I won the French award at my high school because Lester Moroney was a genuine and authentic human being to me, whereas other teachers were not. I think in business, we want to do that. And in the FBI, we do it too. The other interesting thing about the FBI is there's these things called virtual kidnappings that happen, which is your 22 year old daughter is vacationing in Mexico City and you receive a phone call that says, we are watching your daughter. She's in this particular hotel. And if she leaves the hotel, we're going to kill her. You need to send us $50,000 or she's never going to be able to leave that hotel alive. Now, that virtual kidnapping isn't actually happening. What's happened is a bellhop at the hotel has sold your daughter's name to a call center in Tajikistan. meaningful connections to parents because you have to convince a parent to just tell your 22 year old, just go and continue your vacation. You're not in any problem at all. ## [57:52 - 1:00:58]

Second question they asked is, how would you use stories to differentiate a business? For instance, what story would you tell if you were the 21st restaurant in a town selling burgers and fries? Well, that sounds like a bad business model to begin with. But what I would say is, if we're the 21st restaurant in town selling burgers and fries, we're going to have to differentiate ourselves, which is to say, I'm going to drill down into that business until I find the thing that we can tell a story about that makes us different than the other 21 restaurants. If there is nothing to say that is different, I would say that business is already in trouble. And I think every business probably has something that differentiates them, even if it is, I don't know, even if it is van is the way van is the server at my restaurant and van is amazing. So if nothing else, you come and see van and we do a whole campaign around a person that you want to come see in the restaurant, or I'd say, can we do something about your fries that make them different than all other fries? Or I'd have to find something. It's a fair question. If you are just doing what everyone else is doing exactly like they're doing it, it's going to be a lot harder to tell a story. And I would suggest don't open that business unless you find a differentiator in some way. And then it says, what are the proven benefits? What are proven benefits to expand on convenience, novelty, health? Oh, yeah. So like those are things, right? They are convenience. Like maybe your burger place is more convenient. Maybe it's a novel in some way. I was in some upstate New York location doing a solo show and I went to their burger place. And I went to their burger place because it said it was the oldest standing burger place in town. And that was novelty. I was like, okay, I'm going to go to that one. And it had a counter that was like 40 feet long. And I was the only person at the counter, which made me feel really lonely because it was two in the afternoon. But it had a 40 foot long counter that just stretched on forever. And like that was novel to me. And it also had a jukebox and an actual jukebox. So novelty is great. Yeah, if your health is another great example they list here, which is if your burgers and fries are more healthy than other people's, that's great. Actually, if your burgers and fries are not more healthy, that's also great. I might say, listen, I got a burger place and you know what, we don't care about healthy burgers.
